evidence: Docstring states **Fuglede's conjecture** in one dimension, the open one-dimensional case of the conjecture referenced to the Fuglede's conjecture Wikipedia page.
Docstring states **Fuglede's conjecture** in two dimensions, the open two-dimensional case of the conjecture referenced to the Fuglede's conjecture Wikipedia page.
Docstring states **Fuglede's conjecture** in three or higher dimensions has been disproven — the solved case of the same conjecture.
The open conjectured bound |E(r)| = O(r^{1/2+o(1)}) is the Gauss circle problem per the file's Wikipedia reference.
Gauss's proved bound |E(r)| <= 2*sqrt(2)*pi*r on the lattice-point error term of the Gauss circle problem.
Hardy–Landau lower bound on the error term of the Gauss circle problem.
Exact floor-sum formula for N(r), the lattice-point count of the Gauss circle problem.
Docstring: Gilbreath's conjecture states that every term in the sequence d^k_0 for k > 0 is equal to 1, referenced to the Gilbreath's conjecture Wikipedia page.
The iterated differences d start from d^0(n) = p_n, the sequence of prime numbers.
Docstring asks whether every even integer greater than 2 is the sum of two primes, referenced to the Goldbach's conjecture Wikipedia page.
The statement asserts every even n > 2 is a sum of two primes.
Docstring: every odd integer greater than 5 can be written as the sum of three primes — the ternary/weak Goldbach conjecture, solved by Helfgott.
The Ringel–Kotzig conjecture asserts every tree admits a graceful labeling, referenced to Wikipedia/Graceful_labeling; no separate Wikidata item exists for the conjecture itself.
Docstring **Grimm's Conjecture**: k consecutive composites n..n+k-1 admit k distinct primes p_i with p_i | n+i, referenced to the Grimm's conjecture Wikipedia page.
Docstring labels this a weaker version of Grimm's Conjecture (the product of the k composites has at least k distinct prime divisors).
The hypothesis requires n, n+1, ..., n+k-1 to all be composite numbers.

qid_label: Gromov's theorem on groups of polynomial growth
evidence: Docstring **Gromov's Polynomial Growth Theorem**: a finitely generated group has polynomial growth iff it is virtually nilpotent, referenced to the eponymous Wikipedia page.
The equivalence is with Group.IsVirtuallyNilpotent — being virtually nilpotent.
The gap conjecture quantifies over finitely generated groups (S.Finite with Subgroup.closure S = top) of superpolynomial growth.

qid_label: Gromov's theorem on groups of polynomial growth
evidence: The file's reference is the gap-conjecture section of the Gromov's theorem on groups of polynomial growth Wikipedia article; no separate Wikidata item exists for the gap conjecture.
The Hadamard conjecture (a Hadamard matrix exists for every n = 4k) is referenced to the Hadamard matrix Wikipedia article; no separate Wikidata item exists for the conjecture.
Solved cases: a Hadamard matrix of size 4k is known for all k <= 166.
The smallest order with no known Hadamard matrix is 668 = 4*167.
